tasks.March: HAIL WATCH,

tasks.May: Irrigation + fountain turn-on, clean, repair, Pressure washing

tasks.March: Lawn renovations
tasks.May: aerate and overseed. Apply beneficial nematodes when soil temperatures go above 60. Apply to wet lawn and water in.

tasks.March: Irrigation Turn ON

tasks.April: Cut down all stems when spring growth is under way. PRUNE to reduce branch crossing and crowding.
tasks.June: Check for crowding and crossing and remove.

tasks.March: Once buds have swelled, PRUNE all branches to the bud pair closest to the center. Cut all weak, damaged, or crowded canes to the ground.
tasks.October: Leave branches to antique for winter display.

tasks.February: PRUNE (Presidents day). remove all dead branches and any smaller than a pencil. Remove crowding and crossing.
tasks.March: Fertilize.
tasks.September: Leave remaining roses to form hips for winter display and tea.
tasks.November: light prune to protect from wind damage

tasks.February: Remove dead, damaged, diseased, crossing canes. Cut all canes 1" or more in diameter to the ground. Select the 6 strongest new shoots and remove the rest.
tasks.March: Fertilize.
PRUNE for form and airflow.
Prune for shape and to remove any dead or damaged branches. This encourages bushier growth.
tasks.June: Water regularly but ensure good drainage to keep away root rot.
tasks.October: Mulch around the base to help retain moisture and protect roots during the winter.

tasks.May: PRUNE back to two nodes of new growth.

tasks.March: PRUNE back. divide when 3-4 years old.
tasks.April: HARVEST. take the top 5-6 inches just before flowering for best flavor. Leave at least 5" of growth above the wood.
tasks.June: HARVEST and soft PRUNE after blooming
tasks.July: HARVEST and soft PRUNE after blooming
tasks.October: Plant out new starts w2. Hard prune mature plants after first frost.

tasks.January: PRUNE for form when bare and dormant
tasks.March: PRUNE crossing or dense branches when first spring growth appears.

tasks.February: PRUNE crossing, damaged, or dense branches. Prune fruiting branches to closest outward facing nodes.
tasks.March: Spray with Copper Fungicide

tasks.January: Don't panic if the vine loses some or all its leaves in severe cold.
tasks.March: Prune lightly to remove deadwood and shape the vine as desired.
tasks.June: Water regularly to maintain consistant soil moisture.

tasks.March: Remove dead or damaged fronds.
tasks.October: Remove any dead or damaged fronds.

tasks.February: Remove last years fronds when new fronds start to swell at the base.
tasks.March: Remove last years fronds when new fronds start to swell at the base.
Cut back old foliage when new foliage begins to show at the bottom.
tasks.October: Leave seed stems to antique and provide winter interest.

tasks.March: Apply a layer of compost as new growth emerges to provide nutrients and maintain moisture through the summer.
tasks.April: If needed, divide larger plants to rejuvinate growth.
tasks.July: If flagging, water more regularly. It prefers consistently damp soil.
tasks.August: If flagging, water more regularly. It prefers consistently damp soil.
tasks.September: If flagging, water more regularly. It prefers consistently damp soil.
tasks.October: Trim back foliage to 6" to prepare for dormancy. Mulch over root bases to protect from winter temperatures.

tasks.April: If needed, lightly prune out dead or damage branches and to maintain shape.
tasks.July: Water sparingly, alllowing to dry out between waterings.
tasks.October: Mulch ~2" to protect base from winter freeze.

tasks.January: Remove dead sections and runners. Thin healthiest plants to 6" for edible strawberries.
tasks.April: Remove shoots to contain spread and encourage bushy growth.
tasks.June: Remove shoots to contain spread and encourage bushy growth.
tasks.August: Remove shoots to contain spread and encourage bushy growth.
tasks.October: Remove shoots to contain spread and encourage bushy growth.

tasks.May: Cut back generously to encourage bushier growth and continues greenery. Mulch to help retain moisture and protect during hot weather.
tasks.June: Cut and harvest regularly to promote bushier growth and the best flavor.
tasks.July: Cut and harvest regularly to promote bushier growth and the best flavor.
tasks.August: Cut and harvest regularly to promote bushier growth and the best flavor.

tasks.March: Remove damaged or dead foliage to encourage growth and shape.

tasks.April: Prune for shape after flowering is finished.
tasks.October: Reduce watering as weather cools to prepare for winter weather. Mulch around base to retain moisture and protect surface roots from freeze.

tasks.September: Keep debris off of each clump to keep it healthy.

tasks.July: PRUNE back all spent bloom to reinvigorate.

tasks.April: Divide to propogate new plants if desired.

tasks.May: If needed, divide and transplant as soon as you see new growth.
tasks.November: Cut back foliage after first frost.

tasks.September: HARVEST last flowers. Leave some flowers to develop seed. sow any additional seed for new spring additions.

tasks.October: Cut down spent foliage to the ground.

tasks.July: Cut back to reinvigorate bloom and form.

tasks.February: Cut dead stems to the ground and apply mulch.
tasks.June: Deadhead spent flowers to encourage more blooms. Cut stems to the ground after flowering for new coliage growth.
tasks.July: Deadhead spent flowers to encourage more blooms. Cut stems to the ground after flowering for new coliage growth.
tasks.August: Deadhead spent flowers to encourage more blooms. Cut stems to the ground after flowering for new coliage growth.
tasks.September: Divide and transplant older clumping plants to rejuvinate and multiply them.
tasks.November: You can choose to leave fading stems to provide food for winter birds or shelter for pollinators.

tasks.April: After initial bloom, prune spent flowers to encocourage reblooming and a bushier habit.
tasks.June: Deadhead spent flowers to continue bloom.
tasks.July: Deadhead spent flowers to continue bloom.
tasks.August: Deadhead spent flowers to continue bloom.

tasks.March: Remove any damaged or old foliage.
Once goes to seed, cut back
tasks.October: Remove leaves once flowers are 6" or taller.

tasks.March: Divide if overly large or hollowing out in the center. Plant divisions or any new plants.
tasks.May: If flagging, apply a nitrogen rich fertilizer.
tasks.June: Harvest blooms regularly to control self-seeding and to continue bloom. HARVEST blades that are not plant stems for eating.
tasks.July: Harvest blooms regularly to control self-seeding and to continue bloom. HARVEST blades that are not plant stems for eating.
tasks.August: Harvest blooms regularly to control self-seeding and to continue bloom. HARVEST blades that are not plant stems for eating.
tasks.September: Harvest blooms regularly to control self-seeding and to continue bloom. HARVEST blades that are not plant stems for eating.
tasks.October: Apply a thick layer of mulch to protect from winter freeze. If flagging, cut back to a few inches above the ground before first frost.

tasks.February: Mulch with pine straw. Fertilize with blood and bone meal.

tasks.May: plant out new starts. HARVEST established plants just before flowering for best flavor. cut down to 4" to encourage root strength and bushiness. Dry and store.

tasks.February: PRUNE canes that have already fruited all the way to the ground. Remove any new canes that are too crowded. Remove suckers where crowding.
tasks.March: Incorporate balanced fertilizer, leaving 2-3" space around stems to keep from burning the roots
tasks.October: Once harvest is complete, remove all second-year canes (those that fruited), leaving first-year canes to fruit in the fall. Remove any weak canes.
